title: HTTP + SSE Transport
concept: Connecting to a remote server over HTTP with server-sent events for streaming responses.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What transport medium does the 'stdio' MCP transport use to communicate with a server?

options.text: Standard input and output (stdin/stdout) of a local process
Shared memory buffers in the OS kernel

exercises.explain: In the previous lesson, we learned that stdio transport uses the local system's standard input and output channels (stdin/stdout) to pass messages to a local subprocess.

cards.title: Going Beyond Your Machine
cards.body: Local stdio transport is great when the tool runs on your own machine. But what if you want to connect to a shared tool running on a remote cloud server? We need a way to stream data across the web.

cards.title: What is HTTP + SSE Transport?
cards.body: HTTP + SSE transport allows an MCP client to connect to a remote server over the internet. The client receives a continuous stream of events from the server over a single HTTP connection, while sending messages back using standard HTTP POST requests.

cards.title: The Drive-Thru Conveyor Belt
cards.body: Imagine a drive-thru window where you place your order (HTTP POST), and instead of waiting at the window, the kitchen runs a long, flexible conveyor belt (SSE) directly to your car, continuously dropping food items onto it as they are cooked.

cards.title: Remote Tool Integration
cards.body: An AI coding assistant connects to a remote database tool hosted on another server. The assistant opens an SSE connection to watch for schema changes, and sends queries over HTTP POST.

cards.title: It's Just HTTP!
cards.body: You might think SSE requires complex WebSockets. In reality, Server-Sent Events are just standard HTTP connections kept open for streaming text, making them much simpler to set up and scale through web firewalls.

cards.title: Local Pipes vs. Web Streams
cards.body: Compare how stdio relies on local operating system pipes, whereas HTTP + SSE relies on standard web protocols to cross network boundaries.

left.text: Uses stdin/stdout. Requires the server to run locally on your own computer.

right.text: Uses web ports. Connects to tools running anywhere in the world.

exercises.prompt: What is the primary advantage of HTTP + SSE transport over stdio transport in MCP?

options.text: It runs faster by executing local binaries directly.
It allows clients to connect to tools hosted on remote cloud servers.
It completely removes the need for HTTP headers.
It relies on standard input and output pipes.

exercises.explain: HTTP + SSE is designed for remote cloud communication, whereas stdio is limited to local subprocesses on the same machine.

exercises.prompt: Is the Server-Sent Events (SSE) channel in MCP bidirectional, allowing the client to send messages back through the same stream?
exercises.explain: SSE is unidirectional. The server streams events to the client, but the client must send its own requests back using separate HTTP POST requests.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term that represents the streaming channel from the server to the client:
exercises.sentence: In remote MCP, the server pushes events to the client using Server-Sent Events while the client talks back using POST requests.
exercises.explain: Server-Sent Events (SSE) provide a lightweight, unidirectional stream from the remote server to the client, while standard HTTP POST requests go the other way.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes how an MCP client actually connects to a remote server using HTTP + SSE?

options.text: We launch the remote server as a local system process and pipe raw stdin text to its cloud IP address.
We establish a connection to the server's SSE endpoint to listen for messages, and send client requests via HTTP POST.
We compile the remote MCP server into a WebAssembly binary and run it directly in our client's browser local memory.

exercises.explain: The AI option includes the standard elements of remote MCP transport: establishing an SSE endpoint to receive events and using POST for sending JSON-RPC payloads.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following best describes the structural lifecycle of an MCP session running over HTTP + SSE?

options.text: The client establishes a local stdio pipe to execute the remote server's binary.
The client and server open a bidirectional raw TCP socket where both send raw text.
The client receives events via a long-lived GET request (SSE) and sends commands via separate POST requests.
The server initiates a POST request to the client's local port whenever a tool completes.

exercises.explain: HTTP + SSE splits the workload: the client sends commands as standard HTTP POST requests, while the server pushes events back over a single, long-lived SSE connection. This bypasses the need for local subprocesses or heavy bidirectional WebSocket overhead.
